author: @kevinmullner4280
text:
I was eye-balling this exact model for nearly a year. Two months ago german amazon offered it for 499,- €, incl. shipment. But i missed the opportunity😴...and was crying my eyes out. 😭 😅
Now it is around 659,- € again. But then, the unexpected twist came around in form of a CT - 2140 V [one of the many variations of the 210 x 400 mm/ 8 x 16 inch], offered on german ebay, to be delivered to your doorstep for 689,- € [744,- $], available in the complete EU [now 729,- € incl. shipping]. 

It has got 750 w instead of 550 w, a 125 mm [5"] chuck which offers so much more possibilities, it´s got a MT 5 with a 38 mm spindle bore [tailstock is MT 2], a whopping 61,5 kg of "steadfastiness" and - on top of it all:  the concentricity of the spindle is 0,005 mm!!!! - not inch, what would´ve been already great! I couldn´t believe my luck at first and borrowed a Mitutoyo because i thought my Steinle dial indicator had a defect or wasn´t simply good enough. I couldn´t have been farther from the truth...the concentricity is simply that good. 
But, as everybody should know; this machines always come with some issues. To get real professional results out of it you have to clean and deburr them. Luckily mine came without the often used smear out of cheap grease and metal powder leftovers from production/assembly to prevent them from rusting. In my caser, no smear and no rust at all. 
After the "burrclean" you may have to deal with the backlashes and other minor things. But it´s all worth it. 
To be fair: i took a great risk, it is always like a lottery with these babes. The guy that bought one right before me had to sent his back because it did not work at all. I do not think that´s the truth at all. Some people think they will get something with a QC like Precision M., Festool, Milwaukee, Fein, Bosch, DeWalt and other "high end" manufacturers. Which is simply impossible for the given price.
These machines should only be bought by people willing to put time and effort into them and who like a little gamble. But even if you are not as lucky as i was - you can always fiddle this little gems to a degree of precision that lets them shine. 

This is no toy for people with two left hands [no offence here, could be, in some cases, two right hands]. A bit of skill is needed to get into machining. 
If you´ve never worked with a drill press, table saws etc. pp...in short, with electric powered tools...this isn´t the best tool to start a hobby workshop. 
But hey, maybe you´re gifted and you´ll get into it like a champ in a few minutes. For all others [like me]: please be careful.

It´s May the 14th, 2024 now by the way. Good luck in getting one of those golden nuggets i got my hands on.

author: @DougWilson-gp6vs
text: You must have been very lucky, I have had nothing but problems with mine (exact same model ) bought it just over a year ago, used it no more than 6 times. On the seventh switched on and motor ran flat out, speed controller did nothing in fact I had smoke coming from the motor. Got absolutely nothing from Vevor as I purchased this direct from them. Totally ignored any communications fro myself. Would not touch another one of these if it was a gift. Buy cheap and thats what you get. As for support from the manufacturer, it does not exist very dissapointing.

replies.text: A 150mm cross slide dro is likely your best choice, unless you are doing a travel mod to get a bit more working real estate out of the machine.

author: @joshfrentzel6162
text: What quick change tool post fits without modification?

replies.author: @tadhasse
replies.text:
AXA 250-000 or AXA 250-100
The 000 is slightly smaller and will take 12mm tools. The holders are not expensive.. The -100, a touch more beef, but will also cramp your work size with more minimum tool stick out.

author: @ka9dgx
text:
Thread cutting was a huge advance in lathes. Maudslay's Thread Cutting Lathe of 1800 (which you could actually go see in London) is one of the tools that I think was pivotal in the progress of the Industrial Revolution. It's amazing how well a cheap lathe can do the job in minutes that used to represent a week of skilled craft, cutting a screw.

Steel gears are an upgrade. She chose well.

author: @johnnodge4327
text:
You might find the more expensive 8 X 16 lathes have 2 speed gears. 
When I was looking for a mini lathe, there were several iterations of drive to the spindle. 

I've got the 2 speed head stock, which means the chuck speed can be slower, with increased torque.
I also changed the belt drive ratio, again to increase torque, at the expense of spindle speed, which for the most part is excessive if it exceeds 1200 RPM. 
The chuck guard should have an inhibitor switch, which might need adjustment. 

There's plenty of information on improvements for these lathes, but you should consider it an assembled kit, which will need stripping, cleaning, lubrication and reassembly before it performs  at its best. 
Have fun!!
